title: EKS Cluster Zero Downtime Upgrade
content:
EKS cluster upgrades must follow a blue-green node group strategy to ensure zero downtime.

1. Pre-upgrade checks:
   - Run: kubectl get nodes && kubectl get pods --all-namespaces
   - Verify all pods are Running/Ready
   - Check PodDisruptionBudgets: kubectl get pdb --all-namespaces
   - Snapshot all critical PVs before proceeding

2. Upgrade control plane first:
   - Update cluster version in Terraform: cluster_version = "1.31"
   - Run: terraform plan -target=module.eks && terraform apply -target=module.eks
   - Wait for control plane upgrade: aws eks describe-cluster --name CLUSTER_NAME

3. Upgrade node groups (blue-green):
   - Create new node group with upgraded AMI in Terraform
   - Cordon old nodes: kubectl cordon <node>
   - Drain old nodes: kubectl drain <node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
   - Verify pods rescheduled on new nodes
   - Terminate old node group after all pods migrated

4. Update add-ons:
   - aws eks update-addon --cluster-name CLUSTER_NAME --addon-name vpc-cni
   - aws eks update-addon --cluster-name CLUSTER_NAME --addon-name kube-proxy
   - aws eks update-addon --cluster-name CLUSTER_NAME --addon-name coredns

5. Post-upgrade validation:
   - kubectl get nodes -o wide (all nodes should show new version)
   - Run smoke tests on critical workloads
   - Verify Prometheus/Grafana dashboards show no anomalies

title: Karpenter Node Provisioner Configuration
content:
Karpenter replaces Cluster Autoscaler with faster, more flexible node provisioning.

1. Install Karpenter via Helm:
   helm upgrade --install karpenter oci://public.ecr.aws/karpenter/karpenter \
     --namespace karpenter \
     --create-namespace \
     --set settings.clusterName=CLUSTER_NAME \
     --set settings.interruptionQueue=QUEUE_NAME

2. Create NodePool:
   apiVersion: karpenter.sh/v1
   kind: NodePool
   spec:
     template:
       spec:
         requirements:
           - key: karpenter.k8s.aws/instance-category
             operator: In
             values: [c, m, r]
           - key: kubernetes.io/arch
             operator: In
             values: [amd64]
         nodeClassRef:
           name: default
     limits:
       cpu: 1000
     disruption:
       consolidationPolicy: WhenUnderutilized
       consolidateAfter: 30s

3. Cost optimisation with Spot:
   - Add karpenter.k8s.aws/capacity-type: [spot, on-demand]
   - Karpenter automatically falls back to on-demand if spot unavailable
   - Monitor savings: kubectl get nodes -l karpenter.sh/capacity-type=spot

4. Monitoring:
   - Check Karpenter logs: kubectl logs -n karpenter -l app.kubernetes.io/name=karpenter
   - Prometheus metrics exposed at :8080/metrics

title: Terraform State Management and Locking
content:
Terraform remote state with S3 backend and DynamoDB locking prevents concurrent modifications.

1. Backend configuration:
   terraform {
     backend "s3" {
       bucket         = "company-terraform-state"
       key            = "platform/eks/terraform.tfstate"
       region         = "eu-west-1"
       encrypt        = true
       dynamodb_table = "terraform-state-lock"
     }
   }

2. Create DynamoDB table for locking:
   resource "aws_dynamodb_table" "terraform_lock" {
     name         = "terraform-state-lock"
     billing_mode = "PAY_PER_REQUEST"
     hash_key     = "LockID"
     attribute {
       name = "LockID"
       type = "S"
     }
   }

3. Module structure best practices:
   platform-x/
   ├── modules/
   │   ├── eks/
   │   ├── vpc/
   │   ├── iam/
   │   └── argocd/
   ├── environments/
   │   ├── dev/
   │   ├── staging/
   │   └── prod/
   └── README.md

4. Workspace commands:
   terraform workspace new prod
   terraform workspace select prod
   terraform plan -var-file=environments/prod/terraform.tfvars
   terraform apply -auto-approve

5. Drift detection:
   terraform plan -detailed-exitcode
   Exit code 2 = drift detected, run apply to reconcile

title: Multi-Region EKS Deployment with Terraform
ArgoCD Application Deployment
content:
ArgoCD manages application deployments declaratively via Git as the source of truth.

1. Install ArgoCD:
   kubectl create namespace argocd
   kubectl apply -n argocd -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/argoproj/argo-cd/stable/manifests/install.yaml

2. Application manifest:
   apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
   kind: Application
   metadata:
     name: platform-app
     namespace: argocd
   spec:
     project: default
     source:
       repoURL: https://github.com/org/platform
       targetRevision: main
       path: k8s/overlays/prod
     destination:
       server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
       namespace: production
     syncPolicy:
       automated:
         prune: true
         selfHeal: true
       syncOptions:
         - CreateNamespace=true

3. Sync strategies:
   - Manual: argocd app sync platform-app
   - Automated with selfHeal: detects and corrects drift automatically
   - Wave-based: use argocd.argoproj.io/sync-wave annotations for ordering

4. Rollback:
   argocd app history platform-app
   argocd app rollback platform-app REVISION_ID

5. Multi-cluster setup:
   argocd cluster add CONTEXT_NAME
   argocd cluster list

title: Prometheus and Grafana Stack Setup
content:
Full LGTM observability stack: Loki (logs), Grafana (visualisation), Tempo (traces), Mimir (metrics).

1. Install kube-prometheus-stack:
   helm repo add prometheus-community https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts
   helm upgrade --install kube-prometheus-stack prometheus-community/kube-prometheus-stack \
     --namespace monitoring --create-namespace \
     --values monitoring/values.yaml

2. Loki for log aggregation:
   helm upgrade --install loki grafana/loki-stack \
     --namespace monitoring \
     --set promtail.enabled=true \
     --set loki.persistence.enabled=true

3. Key Prometheus alerts to configure:
   - KubePodCrashLooping: pod restart rate > 5 in 15m
   - KubeNodeNotReady: node not ready > 15m
   - KubePVCPendingTooLong: PVC pending > 30m
   - HighMemoryUsage: memory > 85% for 10m
   - APIServerLatencyHigh: p99 latency > 1s

4. Grafana dashboard IDs to import:
   - 315: Kubernetes cluster monitoring
   - 13770: EKS cluster overview
   - 12006: ArgoCD dashboard
   - 13639: Loki log explorer

5. OpenTelemetry instrumentation:
   - Deploy otel-collector as DaemonSet
   - Configure exporters to Prometheus + Loki
   - Auto-instrument apps using otel operator

title: Dynatrace APM Integration with EKS
content:
Dynatrace OneAgent provides full-stack observability for EKS workloads.

1. Deploy Dynatrace Operator:
   kubectl create namespace dynatrace
   kubectl apply -f https://github.com/Dynatrace/dynatrace-operator/releases/latest/download/kubernetes.yaml

2. Create DynaKube resource:
   apiVersion: dynatrace.com/v1beta1
   kind: DynaKube
   metadata:
     name: dynakube
     namespace: dynatrace
   spec:
     apiUrl: https://TENANT.live.dynatrace.com/api
     tokens: dynatrace-tokens
     cloudNativeFullStack:
       image: ""

3. Token requirements:
   - API token with: Read/Write Config, Read/Write Entities, Ingest Metrics
   - Store in AWS Secrets Manager, sync to k8s secret via External Secrets Operator

4. Key dashboards to configure:
   - Service flow map for dependency visibility
   - Problem detection with AI-powered Davis engine
   - SLO tracking: configure availability + response time SLOs

5. Integration with AlertManager:
   - Dynatrace Problems webhook → AlertManager → PagerDuty/Slack
   - Filter noise: set sensitivity to HIGH for production, MEDIUM for staging

title: HashiCorp Vault Secrets Management on EKS
content:
Vault provides dynamic secrets and secret rotation for EKS workloads.

1. Install Vault via Helm:
   helm repo add hashicorp https://helm.releases.hashicorp.com
   helm upgrade --install vault hashicorp/vault \
     --namespace vault --create-namespace \
     --set server.ha.enabled=true \
     --set server.ha.replicas=3

2. Enable Kubernetes auth:
   vault auth enable kubernetes
   vault write auth/kubernetes/config \
     kubernetes_host=https://KUBERNETES_HOST

3. Create policy and role:
   vault policy write platform-policy - <<EOF
   path "secret/data/platform/*" {
     capabilities = ["read"]
   }
   EOF

   vault write auth/kubernetes/role/platform-role \
     bound_service_account_names=platform-sa \
     bound_service_account_namespaces=production \
     policies=platform-policy \
     ttl=1h

4. Inject secrets via annotations:
   annotations:
     vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject: "true"
     vault.hashicorp.com/role: "platform-role"
     vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject-secret-config: "secret/data/platform/config"

5. Secret rotation:
   - Enable dynamic AWS credentials: vault secrets enable aws
   - Vault auto-rotates credentials on TTL expiry
   - Zero secret sprawl: no static credentials in k8s secrets

title: Trivy Container Security Scanning in CI/CD
content:
Trivy scans container images for vulnerabilities in GitHub Actions pipelines.

1. GitHub Actions integration:
   - name: Scan container image
     uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@master
     with:
       image-ref: ${{ env.IMAGE_TAG }}
       format: sarif
       output: trivy-results.sarif
       severity: CRITICAL,HIGH
       exit-code: 1

2. Fail pipeline on critical CVEs:
   - exit-code: 1 fails the pipeline if CRITICAL found
   - Upload results to GitHub Security tab via SARIF upload

3. Scan Terraform IaC:
   trivy config --severity HIGH,CRITICAL ./terraform

4. Scan Kubernetes manifests:
   trivy k8s --report=summary cluster

5. Ignore known false positives:
   .trivyignore:
   CVE-2023-XXXXX  # False positive, upstream fix not available

6. Integration with ECR:
   - Enable ECR enhanced scanning (Trivy-powered)
   - Block deployments if image has unresolved criticals

title: GitHub Actions EKS Deployment Pipeline
content:
End-to-end CI/CD pipeline from code commit to EKS production deployment.

1. Pipeline stages:
   on:
     push:
       branches: [main]
   jobs:
     build:
       steps:
         - uses: actions/checkout@v4
         - name: Build and push Docker image
           run: |
             docker build -t $ECR_REGISTRY/$IMAGE_NAME:$GITHUB_SHA .
             docker push $ECR_REGISTRY/$IMAGE_NAME:$GITHUB_SHA
         - name: Trivy scan
           uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@master
         - name: Update image tag in GitOps repo
           run: |
             git clone https://github.com/org/platform-gitops
             sed -i "s|image:.*|image: $ECR_REGISTRY/$IMAGE_NAME:$GITHUB_SHA|"\
               platform-gitops/k8s/deployment.yaml
             git commit -am "chore: update image to $GITHUB_SHA"
             git push

2. ArgoCD auto-sync picks up the Git change and deploys

3. Rollback procedure:
   - argocd app rollback APP_NAME REVISION
   - Or revert the Git commit and ArgoCD self-heals

4. Environment promotion:
   dev → staging (auto on merge to main)
   staging → prod (manual approval gate via GitHub Environments)

5. Secrets in pipeline:
   - Store as GitHub Actions secrets (never in code)
   - Use OIDC for keyless AWS authentication:
     permissions:
       id-token: write
     - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
       with:
         role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT:role/github-actions-role
         aws-region: eu-west-1

title: AWS VPC Architecture for EKS
content:
Production VPC design for EKS with public/private subnet separation.

1. VPC CIDR allocation:
   - VPC: 10.0.0.0/16
   - Public subnets (3 AZs): 10.0.1.0/24, 10.0.2.0/24, 10.0.3.0/24
   - Private subnets (3 AZs): 10.0.10.0/24, 10.0.11.0/24, 10.0.12.0/24

2. EKS node placement:
   - Worker nodes: private subnets only
   - Load balancers: public subnets
   - NAT Gateways: one per AZ for HA

3. Required subnet tags for EKS:
   Public subnets:
     kubernetes.io/role/elb = 1
   Private subnets:
     kubernetes.io/role/internal-elb = 1
   Both:
     kubernetes.io/cluster/CLUSTER_NAME = owned

4. Security group rules:
   - Node SG: allow 443 from control plane SG
   - Node SG: allow node-to-node communication
   - Control plane SG: allow 443 from node SG

5. VPC flow logs:
   - Enable for all traffic to S3 bucket
   - Use Athena to query: suspicious traffic, port scans
   - Retention: 90 days in S3, 14 days in CloudWatch

title: AWS Cost Optimisation with Cost Explorer
content:
Systematic approach to reducing AWS infrastructure costs.

1. Right-sizing EC2 and EKS nodes:
   - Use Cost Explorer Right Sizing Recommendations
   - aws ce get-rightsizing-recommendation --service EC2
   - Target: CPU utilisation < 40% consistently = downsize

2. Spot instance strategy:
   - EKS non-critical workloads: 60-70% spot, 30-40% on-demand
   - Karpenter handles spot interruptions automatically
   - Typical savings: 60-80% vs on-demand

3. Reserved Instances for baseline workloads:
   - 1-year RI for steady-state EKS nodes
   - Savings Plans for compute-heavy workloads
   - Typical savings: 30-40% vs on-demand

4. Storage optimisation:
   - Move infrequent S3 data to S3-IA or Glacier
   - Delete unattached EBS volumes: aws ec2 describe-volumes --filters Name=status,Values=available
   - Enable S3 Intelligent-Tiering for unknown access patterns

5. Monthly cost review checklist:
   - Unused Elastic IPs: $0.005/hour when unattached
   - Idle NAT Gateways: $0.045/hour
   - Oversized RDS instances
   - Old EBS snapshots
   - Data transfer costs (inter-AZ traffic)

6. Tagging strategy for cost allocation:
   - Required tags: Environment, Team, Project, CostCentre
   - Enforce via AWS Config rules
   - Use Cost Explorer grouped by tags for chargeback

title: Production Incident Response Runbook
content:
Standard operating procedure for production incidents.

1. Severity classification:
   - P1 (Critical): Full outage, data loss risk. Response: 15min. Bridge immediately.
   - P2 (High): Major feature down, >50% users affected. Response: 30min.
   - P3 (Medium): Degraded performance, <50% users affected. Response: 2h.
   - P4 (Low): Minor issue, workaround available. Response: Next business day.

2. Incident declaration:
   - Create incident Slack channel: #inc-YYYYMMDD-description
   - Assign Incident Commander (IC) and Communications Lead
   - Post in #incidents: "P1 declared: [description]. IC: @name. Bridge: [link]"

3. Immediate triage commands:
   kubectl get pods --all-namespaces | grep -v Running
   kubectl get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp | tail -20
   kubectl top nodes && kubectl top pods
   aws cloudwatch get-metric-statistics ...

4. Common incident patterns:
   OOMKilled pods:
     kubectl describe pod POD_NAME | grep -A5 OOM
     Fix: increase memory limits or fix memory leak

   Pending pods:
     kubectl describe pod POD_NAME | grep Events -A20
     Common cause: insufficient node capacity — check Karpenter logs

   CrashLoopBackOff:
     kubectl logs POD_NAME --previous
     kubectl describe pod POD_NAME

5. Post-incident review (PIR):
   - Blameless PIR within 48 hours
   - Document: timeline, root cause, impact, action items
   - Track action items in JIRA with owner and deadline

title: Multi-Cloud Provisioning with Terraform
content:
Unified Terraform platform for provisioning across AWS, Azure, and GCP.

1. Provider configuration:
   terraform {
     required_providers {
       aws   = { source = "hashicorp/aws" }
       azurerm = { source = "hashicorp/azurerm" }
       google = { source = "hashicorp/google" }
     }
   }

2. Abstraction layer pattern:
   modules/
     compute/aws/     # EC2, EKS
     compute/azure/   # AKS, VMs
     compute/gcp/     # GKE, Compute Engine
   interface/
     compute/         # Cloud-agnostic interface module

3. Self-service provisioning portal:
   - Teams submit HCL variable files via PR
   - GitHub Actions validates and plans
   - Auto-applies after approval
   - Reduces provisioning: 6 hours → 30 minutes

4. State isolation per cloud:
   s3://tfstate/aws/PROJECT/terraform.tfstate
   azurerm://tfstate/azure/PROJECT/terraform.tfstate
   gcs://tfstate/gcp/PROJECT/terraform.tfstate

5. Cross-cloud networking:
   - Use Cloudflare or AWS Transit Gateway for cross-cloud connectivity
   - VPN tunnels between cloud VPCs for private routing
   - DNS resolution: Route53 → private hosted zones per cloud
